pr.title: Finish G&W, Ganondorf, Roy, Wireframe Male, and Wireframe Female pointers
pr.author: PsiLupan

summary: Converted a large section of raw fighter data in asm/data.s from hard-coded hexadecimal words and absolute addresses into labeled symbols, .asciz strings, and pointer tables for Mr. Game & Watch, Ganondorf, Roy, Wireframe Male, and Wireframe Female. The PR primarily finishes pointer labeling for costume/joint data, animation .dat file names, and demo lookup strings, improving readability and making later decompilation able to reference data by labels instead of magic addresses.
what_changed: Replaced multiple raw absolute pointer constants such as 0x803D29F8, 0x803D2E80, 0x803D28E8, 0x803D2CD8, 0x803D3280, 0x803D34E8, and related entries with corresponding lbl_803D.... symbols.
Added section comments for pointer table regions, including "Costume and Joint Pointers", "Animation .dat File Name Pointers", and "Demo Lookup Strings".
Decoded raw .4byte ASCII string data into explicit .asciz strings with .balign 4, making file names and resource names visible in source.

For Mr. Game & Watch, introduced labels and strings including "PlGw.dat", "ftDataGamewatch", "PlGwNr.dat", "PlyGamewatch5K_Share_joint", "PlGwAJ.dat", and demo motion strings such as "ftDemoResultMotionFileGamewatch".

For Ganondorf, introduced labels and strings including "PlGn.dat", "ftDataGanon", costume files such as "PlGnRe.dat", "PlGnBu.dat", "PlGnGr.dat", "PlGnLa.dat", joint names such as "PlyGanon5KRe_Share_joint", and demo motion strings such as "ftDemoIntroMotionFileGanon".

For Roy, represented by internal Emblem naming in the excerpt, introduced labels and strings including "PlFe.dat", "ftDataEmblem", "PlFeNr.dat", "PlyEmblem5K_Share_joint", matanim joint strings, color variants such as Re/Bu/Gr/Ye, "PlFeAJ.dat", and demo motion strings such as "ftDemoResultMotionFileEmblem".

For Wireframe Male and Wireframe Female, introduced labeled strings and pointer tables for "PlBo.dat"/"ftDataBoy"/"PlyBoy_Share_joint" and "PlGl.dat"/"ftDataGirl"/"PlyGirl_Share_joint", along with AJ files and intro demo motion file strings.

Added globally visible labels such as lbl_803D28E8, lbl_803D29B8, lbl_803D29C8, lbl_803D2E30, lbl_803D2E40, lbl_803D3498, lbl_803D3558, and lbl_803D35D8 to mark strings and pointer arrays that were previously embedded in anonymous .4byte data.
smart_moves: Preserved binary layout while improving semantics by replacing packed ASCII .4byte sequences with .asciz plus .balign 4 rather than changing the data organization.
Grouped related labels into meaningful asset categories: base data file, ftData name, costume file/joint pairs, animation file name, and demo lookup strings.
Reused newly introduced labels inside pointer tables instead of duplicating absolute addresses, which reduces the chance of stale pointers if labels shift during future data work.

Kept internal asset names exactly as present in the binary, including cases where user-facing character names differ from internal names, such as Roy using Emblem/PlFe and wireframes using Boy/Girl identifiers.
Marked pointer table starts as .global, making them available for later C declarations or cross-file references.
decomp_lessons: When raw data contains recognizable ASCII encoded as .4byte words, convert it to .asciz strings and align explicitly; this makes pointer tables much easier to audit while preserving layout.
For fighter asset tables, label both the strings and the arrays that point to them. The arrays are as important as the strings because many higher-level structures reference the table labels.
Do not rename internal asset identifiers to user-facing character names if the binary uses different resource names; keep names like Gamewatch, Ganon, Emblem, Boy, and Girl as evidence from the data.
Replacing numeric absolute addresses with labels is a low-risk way to improve decompilation readability without changing behavior, especially in asm/data.s where matching depends on exact layout.
Small structural comments in large data blobs help future contributors distinguish costume/joint pointer regions from animation file name pointers and demo lookup string tables.
naming_conventions: Fighter resource files use PlXX.dat and costume suffixes such as Nr, Re, Bu, Gr, La, Ye, and AJ, for example PlGnNr.dat, PlGnRe.dat, PlFeAJ.dat, PlBoAJ.dat, and PlGlAJ.dat.
Data descriptor strings use ftData<Name>, for example ftDataGamewatch, ftDataGanon, ftDataEmblem, ftDataBoy, and ftDataGirl.
Joint strings use Ply<Name>..._Share_joint, with some Roy/Emblem entries also using _Share_matanim_joint.
Demo motion file strings follow ftDemo<Result|Intro|Ending|ViWait>MotionFile<Name>, for example ftDemoResultMotionFileGanon and ftDemoIntroMotionFileGirl.
The PR retained generic lbl_803D.... labels rather than introducing semantic symbol names, so the convention here is address-derived labels with decoded string contents nearby.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Use .asciz for decoded null-terminated strings and follow with .balign 4 to maintain word alignment in data sections.
Convert pointer literals to label references only after defining matching labels at the pointed-to addresses.
For pointer arrays, replace entries like .4byte 0x803D2D14 with .4byte lbl_803D2D14 while preserving NULL sentinel entries and non-pointer constants.
Keep non-pointer binary constants such as 0xFFFFFFFF, 0x01000000, and other structured data words intact when the diff evidence does not establish them as labels or strings.
Introduce .global before newly exposed data labels so other assembly/C translation units can reference them later.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that move these asm/data.s fighter pointer tables into C structs or headers.
Search for semantic renames of lbl_803D29B8, lbl_803D2E30, lbl_803D3498, lbl_803D3558, and lbl_803D35D8.
Look for PRs that document the fighter costume/joint table layout used by PlGw, PlGn, PlFe, PlBo, and PlGl.
Check whether later decomp work keeps Roy as Emblem/PlFe internally or introduces higher-level Roy aliases.
Search for matching tactics around .asciz plus .balign 4 in asm/data.s data cleanup PRs.
